content: Let $$\sigma$$'v and $$\sigma$$'h denote the effective vertical stress and effective horizontal stress, respectively. Which one of the following conditions must be satisfied for a soil element to reach the failure state under Rankine's passive earth pressure condition?
explanation:
Under Rankine's passive earth pressure condition, the failure state of a soil element occurs when the effective horizontal stress ($$\sigma'_{h}$$) exceeds the effective vertical stress ($$\sigma'_{v}$$). This condition describes the scenario where the soil is pushing against a retaining structure, leading to a mobilization of the passive earth pressure.

Therefore, the correct condition is :

$$ \sigma'_{v} < \sigma'_{h} $$

Among the given options, this corresponds to :

Option A

$$ \sigma'_{v} < \sigma'_{h} $$

content:
As per Rankine's theory of earth pressure, the inclination of failure planes is $$\left( {45 + {\phi \over 2}} \right)^\circ $$ with respect to the direction of the minor principal stress.

The above statement is correct for which one of the following options?

options.content: Only the active state and not the passive state
Only the passive state and not the active state
Both active as well as passive state

explanation:
Option C: Both active as well as passive state

In the context of Rankine's earth pressure theory, the failure planes make an angle of $$\left( {45 + {\phi \over 2}} \right)^\circ$$ with respect to the direction of the minor principal stress for both the active and passive states.

In the active state, the wall moves away from the backfill, reducing pressure, and the failure plane forms at this specific angle.

In the passive state, the wall moves into the backfill, increasing pressure, with the failure plane also forming at the same angle.

The angle $$\left( {45 + {\phi \over 2}} \right)^\circ$$ is derived from Mohr's circle representation of stresses, specifically focusing on the principal stress directions when the soil is at failure due to external influences such as retaining walls.

content: The figure shows a vertical retaining wall with backfill consisting of cohesive-frictional soil and a failure plane developed due to passive earth pressure. The forces acting on the failure wedge are: P as the reaction force between the wall and the soil, R as the reaction force on the failure plane, C as the cohesive force along the failure plane and W as the weight of the failure wedge. Assuming that there is no adhesion between the wall and the wedge, identify the most appropriate force polygon for the wedge.

A soil having the average properties, bulk unit weight = 19 kN/m3 ; angle of internal friction = 25° and cohesion = 15 kPa, is being formed on a rock slope existing at an inclination of 35° with the horizontal. The critical height (in m) of the soil formation up to which it would be stable without any failure is _________ (round off to one decimal place).

[Assume the soil is being formed parallel to the rock bedding plane and there is no ground water effect.]
explanation:
Given :

Bulk unit weight, $\gamma = 19 \text{ kN/m}^3$
Angle of internal friction, $\phi = 25^\circ$
Cohesion, $C = 15 \text{ kPa}$
Inclination of the rock slope, $\beta = 35^\circ$
For critical height, the Factor of Safety (FOS) is 1.

For $C-\phi$ soil :

The equation for the Factor of Safety (FOS) is :

$$ \text{FOS} = \frac{C + \gamma H_c \cos^2 i \cdot \tan \phi}{\gamma z \sin i \cdot \cos i} = 1 $$

Substituting the known values :

$$ 1 = \frac{15 + 19 \times H_c \cos^2 35^\circ \cdot \tan 25^\circ}{19 H_c \sin 35^\circ \cdot \cos 35^\circ} $$

Simplifying the above equation :

$$ H_c = 5.03 \text{ m} $$

Thus, the critical height up to which the soil formation would be stable without any failure is approximately 5.0 m.

content: A smooth vertical retaining wall supporting layered soils is shown in figure. According to Rankine’s earth pressure theory, the lateral active earth pressure acting at the base of the wall is ________ kPa (round off to one decimal place).
explanation:
Given :

  • Surcharge load: $ q = 20 \mathrm{kN} / \mathrm{m}^2 $

For Layer 1 :

  • $ \gamma_b = 18 \mathrm{kN} / \mathrm{m}^3 $
  • $ C = 0 \mathrm{kPa} $
  • $ \phi = 32^\circ $

$$ K_{a_1} = \frac{1 - \sin 32^\circ}{1 + \sin 32^\circ} = 0.307 $$

For Layer 2 :

  • $ \gamma_b = 19 \mathrm{kN} / \mathrm{m}^3 $
  • $ C = 20 \mathrm{kPa} $
  • $ \phi = 25^\circ $

$$ K_{a_2} = \frac{1 - \sin 25^\circ}{1 + \sin 25^\circ} = 0.4058 $$

To calculate the active earth pressure at the base of the wall ($ P_a $) :

$$ P_a = (20 + 18 \times 3 + 19 \times 4) \times 0.4058 - 2 \times 20 \sqrt{0.4058} = 35.39 \mathrm{kPa} $$

Thus, the correct answer is 35.4 kPa.

content: A vertical trench is excavated in a clayey soil deposit having a surcharge load of 30 kPa. A fluid of unit weight 12 kN/m3 is poured in the trench to prevent collapse as the excavation proceeds. Assume that the fluid is not seeping through the soil deposit. If the undrained cohesion of the clay deposit is 20 kPa and saturated unit weight is 18 kN/m3 , what is the maximum depth of unsupported excavation (in m, rounded off to two decimal places)? ________________
explanation:
$\gamma_{s u b}=18-12=6 \mathrm{kN} / \mathrm{m}^3$

$\begin{aligned} & P=\sigma_v K_a-2 C \sqrt{K_a} \\\\ & P=\left(30+\gamma_{\text {sub }} z\right) \times 1-2 C \sqrt{1} \\\\ \text { When } \mathrm{P}=0, & 30+6 z-2 \times 20=0 \\\\ 6 z & =10 \\\\ & z=\frac{10}{6} \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

So, maximum depth of unsupported excavation $=2 \times \frac{10}{6}=3.33$

Hence, the correct answer is 3.33 .
